content.text: It started, as these things do, with a flat white. The café near my flat had quietly rewritten its menu: pistachio latte, pistachio croissant, pistachio cookie, a “Dubai chocolate” slice the colour of a tennis ball. The next morning the supermarket had a pistachio aisle-end. A friend pressed a knobbly green-filled chocolate bar into my hand like contraband. I am not, by nature, a man who notices flavours. And even I had noticed.

attrs.alt: Illustration of pistachios, in and out of the shell

content.text: So I did what I always do when the world starts repeating a word at me. I pulled the Google Trends and went looking for the shape of the thing, and, because I cannot help myself, for whether the thing was being
pushed
or
pulled
. Was the world suddenly making too many pistachios and needing somewhere to put them, or suddenly wanting them and scrambling to find enough? Supply, or demand?
The honest answer turned out to be
both, on two completely different clocks

, and the gap between those clocks is the whole story. It is also, once you pull on the threads, a story about a viral chocolate bar, a forty-year-old trade war, a single Iranian seed, and the strange fact that every country on earth caught the same craving in a slightly different way. Let me show you what the data says.
First, the shape of the thing
Here is twenty-two years of worldwide search interest in “pistachio,” with “Dubai chocolate” laid over the top and the key moments of the news cycle marked on it.
Two things jump out, and they pull in opposite directions. The first is the spike everyone remembers: pistachio hit its all-time peak in
March 2025
, the exact month Dubai chocolate crested. If you only looked at the last eighteen months, you would conclude that one viral chocolate bar invented the pistachio craze, full stop.
The second thing undercuts the first. Look further left. Pistachio had
already roughly tripled
off its mid-2000s baseline, from the high teens to the mid-fifties,
before the words “Dubai chocolate” returned a single search.

The line had been climbing, patiently, for fifteen years. And look to the right: a year after the bar peaked, pistachio is still bouncing around in the eighties, near its highs. The spark and the fire are not the same thing.

That is the puzzle in one chart. A decade-long upward grind that no single product explains, and then a near-vertical spike that one product clearly does. To take it apart you have to look at the two clocks separately, the fast one first.
The fast clock: one bar, one phone, one crunch

The spark is almost embarrassingly traceable. In 2021, a British-Egyptian entrepreneur in Dubai named Sarah Hamouda started making a chocolate bar to satisfy a pregnancy craving: milk chocolate stuffed with pistachio cream, tahini and
kataifi
, the shredded filo you find in knafeh. Her company, Fix Dessert Chocolatier, released it in 2022 under the irresistible name “Can’t Get Knafeh of It.”

The economics were deliberately tiny. The bars were handmade and hand-painted, a few dozen a day. Fix sold them only through a delivery app, in Dubai, in a window that opened at 5pm and sold out within minutes, capped at a few hundred orders, at around twenty dollars a bar. Hamouda is
adamant the scarcity was not a marketing ploy

but a consequence of insisting on quality; she has said there was never a real marketing budget. Whether by design or not, the effect was textbook: a product you mostly could not buy is a product everyone wants to talk about.
Then, on
18 December 2023

, a Dubai food influencer named Maria Vehera filmed herself eating one in her car. It was an ASMR close-up, all glossy green ooze and audible crunch. The clip snowballed through early 2024 and now sits north of a hundred million views. There is no evidence anyone paid for that particular video; the breakout looks genuinely organic, which is exactly why it worked. You cannot buy that crunch. You can only make something worth filming and hope.
And here is the part worth dwelling on, because it is the part marketers get wrong. The trend did not stay a bar. It
abstracted into a flavour

. Within months the conversation had detached from Fix entirely and reattached to pistachio itself: pistachio lattes, pistachio croissants, pistachio everything. The viral product became a category input. That abstraction is what turned a Dubai curiosity into a global commodity shock.
Why every brand launched at once (and none of them was early)

One of the questions I kept circling was how the big brands seemed to launch their versions at exactly the same moment, as if someone had fired a starting gun. Lindt, Läderach, Lidl, Aldi, Ritter Sport, Marks & Spencer, Trader Joe’s, Crumbl, Shake Shack: a wall of “Dubai-style” products that all hit between late 2024 and early 2025. Was it coordination? Did somebody spot the trend early?
The unglamorous answer is no on both counts.
They were all reacting to the same public signal, and they were all late.
Fix created the product in 2022. The TikTok lit in December 2023. The big brands did not move until roughly
ten to twelve months later
. Nobody had inside information; they were watching the same view counter you were.
What synchronised them was not a conspiracy but a calendar and a supply chain. The cluster lands on the
Christmas 2024 confectionery season
, the highest-volume gifting window of the year, so every retailer was racing to get product on the holiday shelf at once. And the fastest movers were not the famous brands at all: they were the
discounters’ private labels
. A normal branded confectionery launch takes twelve to twenty-four months. Lidl and Aldi put own-label “Dubai-style” bars on German shelves within
weeks
of peak demand, because a copycat of a known recipe with pre-proven demand skips almost every step that makes new-product development slow.

Which raises the obvious follow-up: did they launch because pistachio supply and price were favourable? No, and this is the causal arrow people reverse. The brands did not ride a cheap-pistachio wave.
Their launches helped cause the price spike.
As the Financial Times reported, chocolatiers were
buying up every kernel they could find
; in the six months to March 2025, Iran shipped about 40% more pistachios to the UAE than in all of the prior year. Demand created the shortage. The shortage did not invite the demand.
The same craving, eight different shapes

You noticed something in the country data that turned out to be the most interesting thing in this whole project: each nation’s curve has its own signature. So I pulled “Dubai chocolate” for eight countries, indexed each one to its own peak, and lined them up in the order they caught it.
Look at
Germany
. It caught the craze weeks before anyone else, in October 2024, exploded almost vertically, and then
crashed

, losing nearly all of it by spring. That is not random. Germany has a large Turkish and Arab diaspora for whom knafeh is not exotic but Tuesday, and the discounters driving the trend, Aldi, Lidl, Ritter Sport, are German. The trend had the shortest distance to travel there, and the German market saturated and moved on faster than anywhere else. (It also produced the only courtroom drama: a
Cologne court ordered Aldi to stop
calling its Turkish-made bar “Dubai” chocolate.)
Now look at
Japan

, at the opposite extreme. Japan barely registered through all of 2025 and then, a full year after everyone else, surged to its peak in early 2026. A slow, late, durable burn: the signature of a market that adopts cautiously and then commits.
Australia
peaked in May, neatly inside its own southern-hemisphere autumn.
France
, a country with a deep, confident pastry culture of its own, gave the trend one polite March spike and then forgot it almost entirely. The home markets, the
UAE
and
Singapore
, held the craving longest, Singapore even mounting a second wave in late 2025.

attrs.headline: A trend is a fluid, and every culture is a differently-shaped glass

content.text: This is the part with the most transfer value for anyone launching globally. There is no such thing as “the” trend curve. The same product, hitting the same planet in the same season, produced a flash-and-crash in Germany, a slow burn in Japan, a polite shrug in France and a year-long plateau in the Gulf. If you plan a worldwide rollout off a single global trendline, you will over-supply the markets that are about to crash and under-supply the ones just waking up. The shape is local even when the spark is global.
There is one more tell hiding in the data. I also pulled
knafeh

, the actual Middle-Eastern dessert the bar is built on. It surged too, peaking alongside the chocolate. People were not only buying a novelty; a good number went looking for the real thing behind it. The countries with the deepest existing knafeh familiarity, Germany and the UAE, had the highest baselines before the craze and, not coincidentally, the fastest reactions to it. Culture is the kindling. Virality is just the match.

None of this is lawless, even if no textbook has a tidy name for it. The shapes map onto a century of diffusion research. Frank Bass’s 1969 model splits any adoption curve into two forces: a coefficient of
innovation
(external push, advertising, a viral video) and a coefficient of
imitation

(plain word of mouth). Germany’s Flash is high-innovation, low-imitation, media-ignited and quick to burn out. Japan’s Slow burn is the mirror image, low-innovation and high-imitation, climbing person by person and so peaking late. A Plateau is a balanced mix with a high ceiling.
The textbook even makes a falsifiable prediction. Cultures high in what Hofstede called
uncertainty avoidance

, their collective discomfort with the untested, ought to be the laggards: Japan, France, Greece and Portugal should hang back while bolder, lower-anxiety markets ignite first. It is a clean, testable claim, so I gathered thirty-two countries and checked it.
It did not hold. Place every market by how fast it peaked and how much of the craving survived, and uncertainty avoidance does
essentially nothing
; its correlation with peak timing is about zero. The tidy predictor flatlines.

What sorts the countries instead is plain geography. Almost every Western market, from Germany to Brazil to the Gulf, did the same thing: a fast spike and a crash back to near nothing, a textbook fad. The exception is a single, startling cluster,
East and Southeast Asia
. Singapore, Japan, Taiwan, Malaysia and Indonesia adopted later and
kept
it, still holding 35 to 46 per cent of their peak a year on. Mexico is the lone Western market that behaved like Asia. Whatever made the craving stick tracks region far better than any culture score.
The
onset
has its own geography. The first markets to catch it were not the boldest but the
closest

: German-speaking Europe, where a Turkish diaspora makes knafeh familiar and where Aldi, Lidl and Ritter Sport are local; Greece and the Balkans, with their Ottoman dessert heritage; and the Gulf itself. Proximity, culinary and physical, lit the fuse; region decided how long it burned.

We did not confirm a textbook here; we broke one. The neat prediction that anxious cultures adopt late did not survive contact with thirty-two countries. What the data does support is humbler and more useful: a trend’s
onset
follows proximity (diaspora, cuisine, local retail), and its
persistence
splits sharply by region, with Asian markets quietly converting a Western fad into something durable. The
fad-versus-trend
distinction (Meyersohn and Katz, 1957) and the
lead-versus-lag market
idea give us the vocabulary; the curves give us the verdict.
There is one last way to look at this, and it is the cleanest. Forget geography, forget culture, and ask only: what
shape
did each curve trace? Cluster all thirty-two countries on shape alone and they collapse into four.

Reassuringly, these are not shapes I invented. There is a quiet literature on the morphology of attention itself. Crane and Sornette, dissecting millions of YouTube videos in 2008, showed that a popularity curve betrays its own origin: an
exogenous
shock, someone with reach sharing a thing, gives a sudden spike that decays fast, while an
endogenous

process of slow word of mouth gives a gentle climb to a late peak. Yang and Leskovec, clustering hundreds of millions of posts in 2011, found that online attention collapses into just six canonical shapes. Our four are a subset of theirs, and they line up with the mechanism: the
Flash
is a pure external jolt that nobody passed on; the
Slow burn
of Japan and Taiwan is the endogenous, word-of-mouth climb; and the
Plateau
is the rare, valuable case of an external spark that then caught and spread on its own.
The slow clock: a quiet empire of trees
While the demand story was loud and sudden, the supply story was silent and a decade in the making. Here is who actually grows the world’s pistachios.
That top line is California. The United States now grows roughly
three times what Iran does

and is still climbing, with a record crop of about 1.57 billion pounds forecast for 2025. The crossover was decisive: the two traded the lead through the 2000s, but 2015 was the last year Iran ever out-grew America. From the 2016 crop the US pulled away for good, its share of world output roughly doubling, from about a third to nearly two-thirds. This is not an accident of one good season; it is the harvest of a planting boom. California’s bearing acreage has multiplied roughly five-fold in twenty years to around 488,000 acres, with another 124,000 acres of young trees not yet in production. The supply was being planted long before the demand arrived to meet it.
But notice the saw-tooth in every line. Pistachio trees
alternate bear

: a heavy crop one year, a light one the next, as the tree rests. It is why 2022 and 2024 sag between the peaks. Across a whole industry this is mostly noise, until the off-years start to line up globally, which is exactly what happened in 2025-26, when Turkey’s crop fell by something like 70% in the same window Iran’s was hit.

attrs.headline: You cannot plant your way out of a viral video

content.text: A pistachio tree takes about
seven years

from planting to a worthwhile crop, and a dozen or more to hit full stride. So the supply that meets a 2024 demand spike was decided around 2017, and the trees going in today are aimed, blind, at the demand of the early 2030s. Agricultural supply is the most inelastic thing in the world on a one-year view. That mismatch, instant demand against seven-year supply, is the engine of the whole boom.
An empire grown from a single Iranian seed

Here is where the supply story stops being agronomy and starts being geopolitics, and where your instinct that there was “a bigger story” turns out to be right. Because California’s pistachio empire is not really Californian. It is Iranian, transplanted.

In 1929, a USDA botanist named William Whitehouse spent six months in Iran collecting pistachios. One selection from an orchard near Kerman, the carpet-making city in Iran’s pistachio heartland, thrived in Chico, California, and was named
“Kerman.”
It became, and largely remains, the genetic backbone of the American crop. The United States did not out-breed Iran. It transplanted Iran’s own tree and then industrialised it.

What turned that head start into a monopoly was a piece of trade history almost nobody remembers. After the 1979 revolution and hostage crisis, and a dumping complaint from American growers, the US slapped a
241% anti-dumping duty
on Iranian in-shell pistachios in 1986. Because the years of outright embargo do not count against its sunset clock, that duty has effectively
walled Iran out of the US market for forty years

, and it was renewed yet again in 2025. As one trade economist put it, the policy was punitive toward Iran rather than designed as industrial strategy, but it functioned as a forty-year shield that handed California its home market during the formative decades.

California then did the strategic work that converts an opening into dominance. A newer, earlier-maturing cultivar (“Golden Hills”) triggered the planting surge after 2012. Branding turned a fiddly nut into a snack: Wonderful Pistachios’ “Get Crackin’” campaign, from Stewart and Lynda Resnick’s
five-billion-dollar agricultural empire

, spent its way to ubiquity. And critically, the Resnicks captured the one input that actually gates Central Valley farming: water. Through the 1990s they gained control of the majority of the Kern Water Bank, a publicly-built underground reservoir, and their farms now drink more water in a year than the city of Los Angeles.

attrs.headline: The honest version of the “water barons” story

content.text: It is tempting to write this as “one family owns California’s water and your pistachios.” Resist it. Fact-checkers have
debunked the strongest version

of that claim; Wonderful grows only about 15 to 20% of the US crop and uses well under 1% of the state’s water. The real story is subtler and more durable: scale, branding, processing and water-security advantages that compound, inside a Valley where the 2014 groundwater law (SGMA) will soon cap new planting and freeze today’s incumbents in place. It is agribusiness power, not a cartoon.

Meanwhile the only rival who could have competed collapsed, largely from self-inflicted wounds. Iran is enduring its worst drought in forty years, with yields in the Rafsanjan heartland reportedly halved and groundwater drawn down far past the safe line. Add sanctions, currency rules that punish exporters, an
EU ban on Iranian pistachios over aflatoxin contamination

in late 2024, and finally the 2025-26 conflict choking exports, and Iranian shipments fell from roughly 175,000 tons in 2024 to around 100,000 in 2025. So when a TikTok detonated global demand, California was very nearly the last reliable, machine-harvested, food-safety-compliant supplier left standing.
That is the bigger story. Not a master plan, but a
compounding of advantages

: an Iranian seed, a punitive forty-year tariff, a water-and-branding machine, and a rival’s drought-and-sanctions collapse, all of which left America holding the world’s pistachio supply at the precise moment the world fell in love with it. The craze didn’t just make California rich. It completed a transfer of culinary ownership four decades in the making. The pistachio is now, improbably, an American crop with an Iranian soul.
So: supply or demand?
There is a cleaner way to see the collision. Plot every crop year as a single point, how much America grew against what its growers were paid, and the story draws itself.

For most of the 2010s the dots obey the oldest law in the book: a big crop pushes the price down (2016, a bumper year, sits near $1.68) and a frost-hit crop sends it up (2015, top-left, $3.29). Then the path bends. In 2023 and 2025 America brought in its
two largest harvests ever
, and yet the grower price climbed, from $1.87 to $2.48. More supply and a higher price, at the same time: the rule broke. How?

Partly because “the price of pistachios” is at least three different numbers, and the craze pulled them apart. The price a grower gets for ordinary in-shell nuts rose only modestly. But Dubai chocolate does not want nuts in a shell; it wants
kernels

, the soft green centres milled into cream, and more specifically peeled, food-grade kernels. That is a narrow, processing-constrained slice of the crop. When demand for it tripled, the bottleneck was not how many trees exist but how fast you can shell and peel them. Handlers ended up cracking lower-grade closed-shell nuts just to get at the kernels inside, and the kernel price detached upward to more than double the in-shell grade.
So the cleanest way I can put it: the
structural
story is supply, a decade of California planting building what is, on a long enough view, a glut. The
acute

2024-26 story is demand, a kernel-specific shock from one dessert slamming into the one part of the supply chain that physically could not flex, made worse by a globally synchronised off-year and a war. The match was demand. The kindling was a supply system that looked abundant in aggregate and was bone-dry exactly where it got hit.

If that pattern sounds familiar, it should. The same year, matcha did the identical thing: a viral green flavour, a processing-constrained input (shade-grown tencha, stone-ground slowly), and a price that jumped not double but
triple
as TikTok demand met a supply that takes a season and a stone mill to expand. Viral demand is a flood. Specialty agricultural supply is a straw. The flood always wins, briefly, and expensively.
Could you have seen it coming?
This is the question I actually care about, because it is the one with transfer value. And the uncomfortable first answer is:
not from the chart you just spent this article looking at
. Google Trends is a
trailing

indicator. People search for a thing after they have already heard of it, tasted it, seen it on a friend’s phone. By the time pistachio is spiking on Trends, it is on the café menu and in the supermarket, and the trade is over.
To check that, I lined up a completely different attention signal against the search line: about two thousand pistachio posts scraped from Reddit, counted by month.
They trace almost the same curve, the correlation is about 0.7, which is reassuring. But notice the forum chatter, if anything,
trails

the search spike rather than leading it. A subreddit is no more a crystal ball than a search box. (With the standard caution that Reddit’s own search over-weights recent months, so the very last stretch is inflated.) The lesson holds either way: the mainstream signals all light up together, and all of them light up late.
The leading signals lived in two quieter places. The first was social chatter among early adopters: the trend-forecasting firm Nextatlas
claims it flagged “pistachio cream” specifically in December 2023

, the exact ingredient Dubai chocolate runs on, months before mainstream search moved. The second was the supply chart itself. A commodity quietly building a structural glut is a coiled spring looking for a demand story; add the cultural priming that was already obvious by 2023, the health halo of nuts, the Instagram-perfect green, Middle-Eastern flavours going mainstream, and you had a market that was over-determined. It did not need Dubai chocolate specifically. It needed
a
spark, and sparks are cheap.
The fad died. The flavour didn’t.
So is it over? The product, yes. The flavour, not even close.

Dubai chocolate, the specific bar, is doing what fads do: down roughly 70% from its peak as the supermarket dupes flood in and the novelty wears off. But pistachio, the flavour, has barely flinched. That divergence is the whole game. The bar was a fad; the flavour graduated into a category.
And the surest sign it has graduated is where the conversation has spread. I pulled a couple of thousand Reddit posts mentioning pistachio and sorted them by community.
Three of the ten busiest communities are not about food at all. They are about
fragrance and makeup
. When a taste jumps from the kitchen into perfume swaps and indie-cosmetics threads, it has stopped being a flavour of the month and become an aesthetic, a thing people want to
smell

like and decorate with. That is what a real category looks like from the inside, and it is why “what’s next” for pistachio is not a single bar but a long tail: pistachio milk, pistachio butter, and a Starbucks Pistachio Matcha Latte that fuses the two reigning green obsessions into one cup. The match burned out. The fire is still going.
What a marketer should take from a nut
If your job is to ride trends rather than supply them, the pistachio has opinions for you.
Forecast the flavour, not the SKU.
Nobody could have predicted “Can’t Get Knafeh of It.” But the
vector

, pistachio, Middle-Eastern, green, nutty-indulgent, was forecastable a year out. The brands that won bet on the flavour and shipped fast; the ones that bet on the specific bar arrived to a price war.
Search is the finish line, not the starting gun.

If you wait until a trend is visible on Google Trends to act, you are buying at the top. The window to win is the gap between the weak signal (early-adopter chatter, a category creeping into adjacent communities) and the spike.
Localise the curve, not just the language.
Germany crashed while Japan was still warming up. A global trend is eight different trends wearing the same coat. Match your supply, spend and timing to each market’s
shape
, not to a worldwide average that describes none of them.
Scarcity is a feature, and abstraction is the goal.
Fix’s sold-out bar manufactured a cult and the dupes did its marketing for free. But the durable prize was never the bar; it was getting the
flavour
to escape the product. Arrive at the abstraction and you build a category; arrive at the saturation and you get markdowns and lawsuits.
And what an operator should take
Instant demand, inelastic supply: respect the gap.

A consumer signal moves in days; a tree moves in years. If your input is agricultural, your nightmare is exactly a TikTok-speed demand shock you cannot answer for seven seasons. Model it before it happens.
Know which SKU the trend actually wants.
The crop was fine; the
kernel

was not. The shortage lived in one processing-constrained grade. Map your bill of materials to the specific input a trend consumes, because that is where the price will detonate, not in the headline commodity.
Concentration plus alternate-bearing equals fragility.

Three countries grow nearly all the world’s pistachios, with biological off-years that can sync up and a war that can sever one of them. Hedge, forward-contract, and hold buffer stock you would normally consider wasteful, because the cost of being caught empty is a competitor’s capture.
What the search graph can, and can’t, tell you
A Trends line is seductive because it looks like truth, but it only measures
attention
, not sales, not supply, not whether anyone actually liked the thing. On its own it would have told me pistachio was popular and left me to guess why.
The trick was never one graph. It was layering several:
attention
against
production
against
price
, and then breaking the attention apart
by country
and
by category

until the single global spike dissolved into eight national curves and a dozen adjacent obsessions. Do that and a trend stops being a mystery and becomes an anatomy: you can see where it came from, whether it is push or pull, who caught it first, and roughly how long it has to run.

The pistachio, it turns out, was never really a surprise. It was a supply chart that had spent forty years quietly loading, a tariff, a drought, a transplanted Iranian tree, all waiting for a story. In December 2023, a woman in a car with a phone handed it one. The only genuinely unpredictable part was which spark, and when. Everything else was sitting in the data, green and patient, the whole time.
